coredata.dc:title: Fine-tuned transfer learning and deep gated recurrent unit methods for state-of-health estimation of the whole life-cycle of lithium-ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Zhenglin
Wang, Jian
Fu, Qiang
Xiong, Ran
Zhang, Sen
Hu, Weihao
coredata.dc:description: Effectively estimating the whole-life-cycle state-of-health (SOH) of lithium-ion batteries is crucial to ensure their safety and reliability. To address this need, this paper proposes a method based on fine-tuning transfer learning and deep gate recurrent unit (DGRU) to estimate the SOH of different types of lithium-ion batteries. Firstly, the DGRU model is pre-trained by extracting highly relevant health indicators (HIs) from random known lithium-ion battery dataset to capture the dynamic characteristics of SOH over aging. Then, the fine-tuning pre-trained model is applied to the dataset of other batteries through transfer learning technology, enhancing the generalization capability of the proposed model on different battery types. Experimental results show that compared with baseline methods such as single-layer gate recurrent unit (SGRU), Gaussian process regression (GPR) and convolutional neural network (CNN), the proposed method has better comprehensive performance on different datasets. Specifically, the mean square error (MSE), mean absolute percentage error (MAPE), and the maximum error (MAXE) of the fine-tuning transfer learning-based deep gate recurrent unit (FTDGRU) model on B0006 cell are only 5.52e−4%, 0.22 %, and 0.0086, respectively, and the MSE, MAPE, and MAXE on CS2_36 cell are only 2.36e−4%,1.93 % and 0.0918, respectively. This method not only improves the accuracy of SOH estimation, but also demonstrates strong adaptability and versatility, making it widely applicable to various lithium-ion battery application scenarios.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
State of health
Deep gate recurrent unit
Fine-tuning transfer learning
Health indicators

Fine-tuning transfer learning for whole-life-cycle SOH estimation
Framework of the proposed FTDGRU estimation model
Analysis of SOH training results in the source domain
Analysis of SOH estimation results in the target domain before and after model fine-tuning
Analysis of SOH estimation results in the target domain using different methods

head.ce:title: Fine-tuned transfer learning and deep gated recurrent unit methods for state-of-health estimation of the whole life-cycle of lithium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: SPIC Southwest Energy Research Institute, Chengdu 610218, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: SPIC Southwest Energy Research Institute, Chengdu, 610218, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Chengdu 611731, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Chengdu, 611731, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: SPIC Sichuan Company, Chengdu 610218, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Effectively estimating the whole-life-cycle state-of-health (SOH) of lithium-ion batteries is crucial to ensure their safety and reliability. To address this need, this paper proposes a method based on fine-tuning transfer learning and deep gate recurrent unit (DGRU) to estimate the SOH of different types of lithium-ion batteries. Firstly, the DGRU model is pre-trained by extracting highly relevant health indicators (HIs) from random known lithium-ion battery dataset to capture the dynamic characteristics of SOH over aging. Then, the fine-tuning pre-trained model is applied to the dataset of other batteries through transfer learning technology, enhancing the generalization capability of the proposed model on different battery types. Experimental results show that compared with baseline methods such as single-layer gate recurrent unit (SGRU), Gaussian process regression (GPR) and convolutional neural network (CNN), the proposed method has better comprehensive performance on different datasets. Specifically, the mean square error (MSE), mean absolute percentage error (MAPE), and the maximum error (MAXE) of the fine-tuning transfer learning-based deep gate recurrent unit (FTDGRU) model on B0006 cell are only 5.52e−4%, 0.22 %, and 0.0086, respectively, and the MSE, MAPE, and MAXE on CS2_36 cell are only 2.36e−4%,1.93 % and 0.0918, respectively. This method not only improves the accuracy of SOH estimation, but also demonstrates strong adaptability and versatility, making it widely applicable to various lithium-ion battery application scenarios.

ce:section.ce:para: With the continuous promotion of the concepts of carbon neutrality and peak carbon peak, electrochemical energy storage, primarily based on lithium-ion batteries, has become a core technology widely applied in the power structure [1–3]. However, the performance and available capacity of lithium-ion batteries gradually decrease over time and frequency of use, which leads to the requirement for highly accurate estimation of whole-life-cycle SOH [4]. Effective whole-life-cycle SOH estimation is one of the key functions of the battery management system (BMS). It reflects the battery energy performance and indicates whether the battery needs to be retired [5,6]. In order to adapt to the rapid development of artificial intelligence (AI) in the field of battery state estimation, cloud-based BMS (C-BMS) is gradually being used in related AI-based methods [7,8]. The motivation for this study is that some existing SOH estimation methods have low accuracy and are not oriented to the whole-life-cycle period. The SOH estimation method developed in this paper can be applied to a variety of lithium-ion battery charging and discharging scenarios.

Traditional SOH estimation methods include coulomb counting, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and physical model-based methods [9–12]. Although these methods can reflect the battery SOH to a certain extent, they usually require a large amount of experimental data and are difficult to adapt to different types of batteries and changing working conditions. With the development of machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) technologies, data-driven methods have gradually become the mainstream direction of battery SOH estimation [13,14]. Common data-driven methods include methods based on feedforward neural networks (FFNNs), methods based on recurrent neural networks (RNNs), methods based on CNNs, and methods based on Gaussian process regression models (GPRs) [15–17]. In the field of SOH estimation, Pan et al. input the terminal voltage during constant current charging into a FFNN to estimate SOH [18]. Bockrath et al. input the directly measured charging-discharging data into the temporal CNN to obtain SOH estimation results [19]. Wang et al. created a composite kernel function with automatic relevance detection approach based on the benchmark GPR to estimate SOH [20]. Liu et al. proposed the SOH estimation approach based on transfer learning-based GPR, and validated its effectiveness under the battery operation data of 700 electric vehicles (EVs) [21].

Notably, due to the powerful capability of RNNs and their variants in handling time series data, they hold a significant position in the field of SOH estimation. Traditional RNNs face issues like vanishing or exploding gradients when processing long sequence data. To address these problems, long short-term memory (LSTM) and gated recurrent unit (GRU), two improved RNN networks, have been widely adopted [22,23]. An improved LSTM variant which is called active states tracking-LSTM has been proposed in the reference [24] for estimating the SOH of lithium-ion batteries. Van et al. proposed a deep learning LSTM-based SOH estimation method with using current, voltage, temperature, solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) resistance and charge transfer resistance as HIs [25]. Ma et al. developed a novel differential evolution grey wolf optimizer-long short-term memory (DEGWO-LSTM) model with 15 HIs extracted from charging-discharging process for estimating SOH [26]. GRU has fewer parameters than LSTM. Therefore, it is more efficient in training and prediction, and is more suitable for battery whole-life-cycle SOH estimation scenarios that requires a lot of calculations [27,28]. Kim et al. combined electrochemical degradation indicators, LSTM and transfer learning for SOH estimation [29]. With extracting multiple HIs, reference [30] adopted transfer learning and deep belief network (DBN)-long short-term memory (LSTM) hybrid network.

Hong et al. proposed a SOH estimation method for battery system in real-world vehicles based on gated recurrent unit neural networks [31]. Reference [27] investigated accurate SOH estimation with a temperature prediction method based on one extreme learning machine. Reference [32] tested the performance of GRU in estimating SOH under diverse temperatures, demonstrating its outstanding results. By introducing a gating mechanism, GRU effectively alleviates the problem of long-term dependence and can more accurately capture the dynamic characteristics of battery aging. However, due to the diversity and complexity of battery data, it is difficult to achieve ideal results in all cases by relying solely on GRU, so a deep framework is introduced in this paper to improve model flexibility. In addition, due to differences in battery types and working conditions, whole-life-cycle SOH estimation without transfer learning and fine-tuning strategies is quite inaccurate, which is also a research gap that needs to be addressed. How to further improve the estimation accuracy and universality of the model is a hot topic in the current research stage.

This paper proposes a fine-tuning transfer learning-based deep gate recurrent unit method method for whole-life-cycle SOH estimation of different lithium-ion batteries. The DGRU model is pre-trained by extracting highly relevant HIs from known lithium-ion battery dataset. Subsequently, the fine-tuning strategy is applied in transfer learning, and some new battery charging-discharging dataset to be tested is input into the updated model to monitor the estimation results of whole-life-cycle SOH. Based on the strong processing performance of the GRU model for long time series, the high flexibility of the deep framework, and the effective improvement capabilities of fine-tuning transfer learning, this paper aims to develop a novel whole-life-cycle SOH estimation method. The main contributions are as follows:

A deep learning framework is established to enhance the flexibility of the proposed model in internal feature extraction and nonlinear relationship processing. This framework is integrated into GRU to further enhance the capability to handle long-term data dependencies.

Fine-tuning transfer learning technique is applied. The pre-trained model is modified by using a small amount of data from new batteries to enhance the generalization capability of the model on different batteries.

The FTDGRU model is developed to obtain the whole-life-cycle SOH estimation results of lithium-ion batteries. Compared with the existing benchmark ML methods, the proposed method shows higher SOH estimation accuracy under MSE and MAPE indicators under the same dataset.

The rest of this paper is arranged as follows: Section 2 conducts the mathematical analysis, including extraction of highly correlated HIs, DGRU modeling, and fine-tuning transfer learning. The description of dataset, correlation analysis results, and SOH estimation results are demonstrated in Section 3. Finally, Section 4 introduces the conclusions and prospects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of HIs and correlation analysis method
ce:section.ce:para: In order to better process lithium-ion battery aging data and optimize the input selection of the estimation model, efficient data can be screened by analyzing the external characteristics of the collected data curves [33]. In this paper, four different HIs are used to track the changes of whole-life-cycle SOH. These four HIs are constant current charging time (CCCT), constant voltage charging time (CVCT), area enclosed by the discharging voltage curve and the x-axis (AreaU), and area enclosed by the discharging current curve and the x-axis (AreaI). Taking the data of the B0005 cell in one charging-discharging cycle provided by NASA as an example [34], Fig. 1 shows the specific meanings of these HIs, which provide key insights and data support for analyzing the battery aging process.

The extracted HIs are processed by min-max normalization. After processing, in order to analyze and prove that they are strongly correlated with the whole-life-cycle SOH, this paper uses the Pearson correlation coefficient value to express the correlation between HIs and the corresponding SOH sequence. This value is obtained by the Pearson correlation analysis method, and the relevant calculation expression is shown in Eq. (1).
(1)
R
X , Y
=
∑
i = 1
n
(
X
i
−
X ¯
)
(
Y
i
−
Y ¯
)
∑
i = 1
n
(
X
i
−
X ¯
)
2
∑
i = 1
n
(
Y
i
−
Y ¯
)
2
Wherein, R is the Pearson correlation coefficient, X and Y represent HIs and SOH after processing, respectively.

The DGRU is an advanced DL model suitable for time series data processing [35]. By stacking multiple GRU layers, DGRU achieves deeper feature extraction and can better capture complex time dependencies. In the estimation of the whole-life-cycle SOH in lithium-ion batteries, the DGRU model uses its powerful time series processing capabilities to provide accurate and reliable estimation results.

In order to understand the working principle of DGRU model, it is needed to master the basics of RNN and GRU. RNN is a type of neural network suitable for processing sequence data. Its basic feature is to capture the temporal dependencies in the sequence by connecting data at different time steps through hidden states. However, RNN is prone to gradient vanishing and gradient exploding problems when dealing with long-term dependencies, which limits its performance. GRU is an improved RNN that alleviates the problem of gradient vanishing or exploding by introducing an update gate and a reset gate. These two gates are used to control the flow of information, thereby effectively managing information transmission and state updates.

One GRU model consists of an input layer, a hidden layer, and an output layer. Its structure includes an update gate and a reset gate. The update gate determines the degree of retention of the state information of the previous time step in the current time step, and the reset gate controls the impact of the state information of the previous time step on the current state [36]. The mathematical expression of the GRU forward propagation is shown in the Eq. (2).
(2)
{
z
t
= σ
(
W
z
⋅
[
h
t − 1
,
x
t
]
)
r
t
= σ
(
W
r
⋅
[
h
t − 1
,
x
t
]
)
h ˜
t
=
tan
h
(
W
h
⋅
[
r
t
⊙
h
t − 1
,
x
t
]
)
h
t
=
(
1 −
z
t
)
⊙

t − 1
+
h ˜
t
⊙
z
t
y
t
= ∂
(
W
y
h
t
+
b
y
)
Wherein, z
t is the update gate output, r
t is the reset gate output, h
t is the hidden state of the current time step, x
t is the input of the current time step, ⊙ represents element-wise multiplication, σ is the sigmoid activation function, tanh is the hyperbolic tangent activation function, and ∂ is the linear activation function. In addition, W
z , W
r , W
h , and W
y correspond to the weights of the update gate, reset gate, intermediate output layer, and output layer, respectively.
h ˜
t
and
h
t
are the intermediate hidden state and hidden state, respectively. Y is the model output, and B
y is the bias of the output layer. DGRU forms a deep network structure by stacking multiple GRU layers to achieve more complex feature extraction and capture of temporal dependencies. The structure of DGRU is composed of multiple layers of GRU units stacked together, and the output of each layer serves as the input of the next layer. This design enables the model to capture temporal dependency information at different levels, thereby improving the predictive and generalization capabilities. Therefore, in DGRU, starting from the second layer, the mathematical expression of the hidden state of each layer of the network is updated as shown in Eq. (3).
(3)
{
h
t
1
= G R U
(
x
t
,
h
t − 1
1
)
h
t
2
= G R U
(
h
t
1
,
h
t − 1
2
)
⋮
h
t
l
= G R U
(
h
t
l − 1
,
h
t − 1
l
)
⋮
h
t
L
= G R U
(
h
t
L − 1
,
h
t − 1
L
)
Wherein, L is the number of layers in the DGRU framework, and
h
t
l
is the hidden state of the l-th layer at time step t. At each time step, the input data passes through the first GRU layer, then passes layer by layer, and finally outputs at the last layer. Each layer has the ability to capture temporal dependencies at different levels, which can be combined to provide a comprehensive understanding of sequence data. The gating mechanism of GRU effectively alleviates the gradient vanishing problem, allowing deep networks to be trained more successfully. The deep framework allows the model to extract richer feature information from data, which helps to improve the accuracy and generalization capability of the model. By combining the deep framework and the GRU model, DGRU has the ability to capture complex temporal dependencies and improve model performance. The proposed DGRU estimation structure is shown in Fig. 2.

Since battery whole-life-cycle SOH estimation is a regression problem, a linear activation function can be used to complete a simple nonlinear mapping of the output layer. In addition, the dropout link is embedded in each layer of the DGRU structure to avoid overfitting as much as possible and enhance the generalization capability of the network. During the training process, this link randomly ignores some neurons to reduce the dependence of network on specific neuron weights.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fine-tuning transfer learning for whole-life-cycle SOH estimation
ce:section.ce:para: In the whole-life-cycle management of batteries, it is crucial to accurately estimate the SOH. SOH directly affects the performance, safety, and service life. However, traditional SOH estimation methods usually rely on a large amount of historical data for model training, which is often impractical in applications, especially for new batteries. Therefore, a fine-tuning transfer learning strategy is adopted to address the challenge of whole-life-cycle SOH estimation in this research.

Transfer learning is a ML method that improves the performance of target domain tasks by leveraging knowledge from the source domain. In this study, the source domain refers to the data of known lithium-ion batteries, and the target domain refers to the data of lithium-ion batteries to be estimated. Specifically, a DGRU model is trained on the source domain data and is fine-tuned by using the target domain data, so that the model can better adapt to the target domain. The core idea of transfer learning is to leverage the rich information and structure of the source domain to make the model perform better on the target domain. This process can be divided into two main steps: pre-training and fine-tuning. First, pre-training is performed on the source domain to obtain an initial model with good generalization capability. Then, the model is fine-tuned by using the target domain data, so that it can better adapt to the characteristics of the target domain.

Fine-tuning is a key step in transfer learning, which aims to adapt the pre-trained model with a small amount of target domain data. The specific implementation process of the fine-tuning strategy is as follows:
Firstly, a DGRU model on the source domain is trained for obtaining the initial model.

Secondly, the parameters of the pre-trained model are used as the initial parameters of the fine-tuning strategy. In this way, the model after fine-tuning has the knowledge and patterns of the source domain at the beginning, so that it can adapt to the target domain data more quickly.
Lastly, in the fine-tuning process, the goal is to minimize the loss function on the target domain data.

The basic idea of fine-tuning is to use the trained parameters in the source domain as the initial values, and then use the target domain data for retraining [37]. This process can be achieved by optimizing the objective loss function, as shown in Eq. (4).
(4)
θ
*
=
arg
min
θ
L
(
θ ;
D
t
)
Wherein, θ* is the set of best model parameters obtained after fine-tuning optimization, θ is the set of model parameters, L is the loss function, and D
t is the target domain data. In the fine-tuning process, the model is first trained with the source domain data to obtain the initial parameter set θ 0, and then the model parameters are adjusted by minimizing the loss function L on the target domain data D
t , so that the model can better adapt to the target domain data. In this way, the model can retain the knowledge of the source domain while making adaptive adjustments on the target domain. Based on the above fine-tuning transfer learning technique, the adaptability of the DGRU model for whole-life-cycle SOH estimation in the target domain is improved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Framework of the proposed FTDGRU estimation model
ce:section.ce:para: This study uses a GRU as the baseline artificial intelligence model and integrates a deep learning framework with a fine-tuning transfer learning technique to construct the FTDGRU neural network for estimating the whole-life-cycle SOH of lithium-ion batteries. The overall architecture of the proposed method is shown in Fig. 3.
As shown in Fig. 3, the main contents of this method include the following four parts:
The first is acquisition of current and voltage data during the cyclic charging-discharging of lithium-ion batteries. This step is the basis for the subsequent analysis of HIs.

The second is extraction of HIs and analysis of their correlation with SOH. HIs is the input parameters of the subsequent ML model, and their effective extraction is crucial to the accuracy of the SOH estimation results.

The third is construction of FTDGRU model. This step includes pre-training the model by using lithium-ion battery source domain data and fine-tuning the transfer learning model by using limited target domain data. Based on the fine-tuning transfer learning technique, the large estimation error caused by the distribution differences of different types of lithium-ion batteries or under different working conditions can be effectively avoided.
The last is performance analysis. The proposed estimation results are evaluated to analyze the adaptability of the FTDGRU model in this research scenario.

The lithium-ion battery datasets used in this study are derived from the available charging-discharging experiment data publicly provided by NASA and the University of Maryland [34,38]. The data of B0005, B0006, and B0029 are from NASA dataset, while the data of CS2_33 and CS2_36 are from the dataset of the University of Maryland. Specifically, the B0005 cell data is used as the source domain data, representing batteries cycled at a constant temperature of 24 °C with a constant current (CC) and constant voltage (CV) charging regime, followed by constant current discharge cycles. For the target domain, the data of B0006, CS2_33, and CS2_36 cells were cycled under similar temperatures and CCCV conditions but with variations in the initial capacity, while the data of B0029 was cycled under a constant temperature of 43 °C and CCCV condition.

In this study, the Pearson correlation coefficient [39] is adopted to describe the correlation between the extracted HIs and the whole-life-cycle SOH. The Pearson correlation coefficients between the HIs and the corresponding SOH of B0005, B0006, B0029, CS2_33 and CS2_36 cells are shown in Fig. 4.

As shown in Fig. 4, the correlations between different HIs and the whole-life-cycle SOH are generally high under different cells. Therefore, the extracted HIs are suitable as the input of the FTDGRU model.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of SOH training results in the source domain
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the number of deep framework layers is 5, the number of training times is 1000, the optimization algorithm used in model training is Adam, the dropout value is 0.2, the learning rate is 0.0001, the hidden size is 64, and the loss function is MSE function. The above parameter adjustment scheme is based on the training loss of source domain [40]. The loss curve and SOH estimation results in the source domain are shown in Fig. 5.

In all figures of this paper, ref represents the reference SOH. As can be observed from Fig. 5, in the source domain, the DGRU model exhibits a very small training loss when estimating the whole-life-cycle SOH, which proves that the proposed model has a high adaptability in this scenario and is suitable for subsequent fine-tuning transfer learning operations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of SOH estimation results in the target domain before and after model fine-tuning
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the DGRU pre-trained model is fine-tuned using the data of the first 3 % cycles of the target domain data. The whole-life-cycle SOH estimation results of the B0006 cell, B0029 cell, CS2_36 cell, and CS2_33 cell before and after model fine-tuning are shown in Fig. 6.

The aging condition of a cell depends on its type and working conditions [41]. To analysis the performance of diverse approaches, MSE, MAPE and MAXE are adopted. The calculation expressions are shown in Eq. (5).
(5)
{
M S E =
1 n
∑
i = 1
n
(
y
i
−
y ˆ
i
)
2
M A P E =
100 %
n
∑
i = 1
n
|
y
i
−
y ˆ
i
y
i
|
M A X E =
max
(
|
y
i
−
y ˆ
i
|
)
Wherein, n represents the cycle number, y
i denotes the i-th actual value, and
y ˆ
i
is the i-th estimated value. In the target domain, the comparison of SOH estimation evaluation indicators before and after fine-tuning is shown in Table 1.

In Table 1, the best estimation results of each cell in MSE, MAPE, and MAXE are highlighted in bold. It can be clearly seen from Table 1 that the fine-tuning transfer learning FTDGRU model performs much better than the transfer learning deep gate recurrent unit (TDGRU) model in the target domain SOH estimation. This is because, without using the fine-tuning strategy, directly applying the pre-trained model obtained from the source domain data to the target domain can lead to domain distribution shift, resulting in poor estimation results. Correspondingly, the FTDGRU model helps to eliminate the impact of domain shift as much as possible to enhance the estimation performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of SOH estimation results in the target domain using different methods
ce:section.ce:para: This study compares the whole-life-cycle SOH estimation performance of the proposed model with benchmark models such as SGRU, GPR, and CNN to highlight the comprehensive advantages of the developed method. It is worth noting that the DGRU, SGRU, GPR and CNN models here all undergo the fine-tuning transfer learning process. At the epoch number of 50, the comparison is conducted in the target domain. The SOH estimation results based on SGRU, GPR, CNN and DGRU are shown in Fig. 7.
After fine-tuning transfer learning, the comparison of SOH estimation evaluation indicators of SGRU, GPR, CNN and FTDGRU in the target domain is shown in Table 2.

Similarly, in Table 2, the best estimation results of each cell in MSE, MAPE and MAXE are highlighted in bold. It can be observed that the estimation results based on SGRU, GPR and CNN have large errors, while the proposed FTDGRU method is significantly better than the other three ML methods in both MSE, MAPE, and MAXE evaluation indicators. Specifically, the MSE, MAPE, and MAXE of the proposed estimation method in B0006 cell are 5.52e−4%, 0.22 %, and 0.0086, respectively. The MSE, MAPE, and MAXE of the proposed estimation method in B0029 cell are 1.62e−3%, 0.29 %, and 0.0165, respectively. In addition, the MSE, MAPE, and MAXE of the proposed estimation method in CS2_36 cell are 2.36e−2%, 1.93 %, and 0.0918, respectively, while the MSE, MAPE, and MAXE of the proposed estimation method in CS2_33 cell are 3.42e−2%, 2.03 %, and 0.1831, respectively. Additionally, the comparison of SOH error indicators for the proposed method and existing advanced algorithms is shown in Table 3.

In Table 3, S1 is the algorithm based on incremental energy analysis and bidirectional LSTM, S2 is the algorithm based on gradual decreasing current, double correlation analysis and GRU, while S3 is the algorithm based on incremental energy per SOC and LSTM-reduction. As can be seen from Table 3, although the proposed method cannot achieve the optimal performance of MSE, MAPE and MAXE at the same time under some cells, its comprehensive performance is the most superior. The experimental results verify the strong adaptability and high accuracy of the proposed method in whole-life-cycle estimation.

Effective whole-life-cycle SOH estimation of lithium-ion batteries has a positive effect on the development of the new energy field. This study constructs a whole-life-cycle SOH estimation method based on fine-tuning transfer learning and DGRU model. Four HIs that are strongly correlated with SOH are proposed as input parameters of the estimation model. The DGRU is used to learn the nonlinear mapping relationship between HIs and SOH. In addition, the fine-tuning transfer learning technique is introduced to effectively solve the distribution shift problem between the source domain data and the target domain data. Under the B0006 and B0029 cells in the NASA dataset and the CS2_36 and CS2_33 cells in the University of Maryland dataset, the experimental results show that the developed SOH estimation method has better results in both MSE, MAPE, and MAXE than the approach before fine-tuning and several ML methods. The limitations of this work include the failure to verify high real-time performance and the failure to consider larger lithium-ion battery structures. Future work can be carried out in the following studies:

Firstly, Research on analyzing the whole-life-cycle SOH estimation performance of lithium-ion battery packs under the proposed method. Secondly, Research on studying the real-time performance of the FTDGRU model in whole-life-cycle SOH estimation.

Jian Wang: Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zhenglin Guo: Resources, Project administration, Methodology. Weihao Hu: Visualization, Project administration, Formal analysis. Sen Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision. Ran Xiong: Writing – original draft, Software, Data curation. Qiang Fu: Validation, Supervision, Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the Sichuan Science and Technology Program of Sichuan Company of State Power Investment Corporation Limited (grant number: XNNY-WW-KJ-2023-03).
